docs.title: Hands-on Kubernetes Notes
docs.text: Welcome to my Kubernetes practice notes. Everything here is organized section-wise with simple explanations and examples.

docs.title: Remarks
docs.text:
--> client version and server version should be the same 

    brew install fish

docs.title: Pods
docs.text:
Pods are just the wrappers for containers

Generally its always one container per pod , but genrally few times its more as a helper container like ingesting logs or metrics of the main container. eg:

docs.title: Writing a Pod Spec
docs.text: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: myapp-pod labels: app: myapp spec: containers: - name: nginx

docs.title: writing pod file for the course
docs.text: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: webapp spec: containers: - name: webapp image: richardchesterwood/k8s-fleetman-webapp-angular:release0

docs.title: Error faced  Pod status: CrashLoopBackOff
docs.text:
Kubernetes starts your container, it crashes/exits, and Kubelet keeps trying to restart it, but each time it fails again, so Kubernetes backs off (waits longer and longer) before the next restart.

RCA - Got this error coz the image didn't supported the same architecture.

Solution: got into the docker hub of it and found the right multi arch image and updated the pod file.

docs.title: Exec into the pod
docs.text:
Since its a single container pod we can exec into it using below command Note: use the Help if not sure about the command

k exec -it <pod Name> -- Command (eg: /bin/bash)

k exec -it webapp -- Date
k exec -it webapp -- bash

docs.title: Concept of labels and Selectors
docs.text:
Labels are key/value pairs that are attached to objects, such as pods. Labels are intended to be used to specify identifying attributes of objects that are meaningful and relevant to users.

Selectors are used to filter a list of resources based on their labels. They allow users to select a subset of resources that match certain criteria.

docs.title: Services
docs.text:
--> service is a long running object with long stable running port.

apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: #Unique key of the service name: flettman-webapp-service spec: # This defines which pods are gonna be represented by this service # The service becomes a endpoint to access the pods / or for other # # services to access the pods selector: app: web#app

ports:
- name: http
  protocol: TCP
  port: 80            # Port on which the service is exposed
  targetPort: 80     # Port on the pod to which the traffic is 
  nodePort: 30080  # Port on each node to expose the service (only for NodePort type)

type: NodePort #second P is capital

docs.title: Type of service
docs.text: ClusterIP - default type - only accessible within the cluster

docs.title:
NodePort - accessible outside the cluster using : 
Nodeport has some conditions
It must be greater than 30000 and less than 32767

--
Why your pod didn’t come back
docs.text:
1. The pod labels do not match the Service selector

    • Ensure that the labels defined in your pod specification match the selector criteria specified in your Service definition. If they do not match, the Service will not be able to route traffic to the pod.

    kubectl get svc -o yaml

    kubectl get pods --show-labels

docs.title: To check the basic status of minikube
Basic Release-based Deployment Strategy
docs.text: A release-based deployment strategy involves deploying new versions of an application based on predefined release cycles. This approach allows for planned updates, ensuring that new features and bug fixes are introduced in a controlled manner.

docs.title: Baisc Way:
docs.text: --> Add a Label of for Release Version to the Deployment --> then Add that label to the Service's selector
